project_identification.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.waterways_within_2_miles: American River (adjacent to northern boundary); Morrison Creek (0.8 miles south)
project_location.railways_within_2_miles: Union Pacific Railroad main line (1.6 miles north)
project_location.schools_within_2_miles: Riverbend High School, Sierra Oaks Elementary School, American River Community College Satellite Campus

development_type.recreational_description: Approximately 17.2 acres of neighborhood and community-serving parks, including a 10-acre riverfront community park with multi-use turf fields, playgrounds, picnic areas, a small amphitheater, and a river overlook, as well as two 3–4 acre neighborhood parks with play structures, half-court basketball, and shaded seating areas. A Class I multi-use trail will run along the American River frontage and connect to the regional trail network.
development_type.transportation_type: Construction of a new collector roadway (Riverbend Parkway extension), new local residential streets, roundabouts at two key intersections, Class II bike lanes on interior streets, and a new signalized intersection at Watt Avenue and Riverbend Parkway.
development_type.other_development_type: Neighborhood-serving civic plaza of approximately 1.5 acres with a small community meeting room (up to 4,000 square feet) and flexible space for a weekly farmers market and seasonal community events.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and equitable access to parks and services; construction-period impacts including dust, vibration, and worker parking; and consistency with regional Sustainable Communities Strategy under SB 375.

land_use_and_project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des: The approximately 118.4-acre project site is currently designated in the City of Riverbend General Plan as Urban Reserve (UR) and Parks/Open Space (P/OS) along the American River frontage. Existing zoning is primarily AG-20 (Agricultural, 20-acre minimum) with a small portion mapped as OS (Open Space) adjacent to the river levee. The property is largely undeveloped, consisting of fallowed pasture previously used for seasonal cattle grazing, several informal dirt access roads, and a cluster of three vacant single-story outbuildings (former agricultural sheds) near the southwest corner of the site. Surrounding land uses include single-family residential neighborhoods to the south and west, the American River Parkway and associated riparian habitat to the north, and a mix of light industrial and commercial uses along Watt Avenue to the east. There are no existing municipal utilities on the site, though water and sewer mains are present in adjacent public rights-of-way along Watt Avenue and Riverbend Parkway.
land_use_and_project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan ("project") proposes to transform an approximately 118.4-acre underutilized agricultural parcel along the American River in the City of Riverbend into a compact, walkable, mixed-use neighborhood with a range of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, office space, and a connected system of parks, open space, and multi-use trails.

At a high level, the project would establish a new Specific Plan and associated rezoning to allow up to 486 residential units, approximately 120,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial/retail uses, approximately 85,000 square feet of office space, and roughly 17.2 acres of public parks and open space, including a 10-acre riverfront community park. The project is intended to implement the City’s General Plan policies promoting infill development, riverfront access, and multimodal transportation choices.

RESIDENTIAL COMPONENT
The residential program is organized into several distinct planning areas to provide a variety of housing types and price points:

• Low-Density Residential (LDR): Approximately 32.5 acres are designated for detached single-family homes at an average density of 5–7 dwelling units per acre, yielding an estimated 210 units. Typical lot sizes would range from 4,000 to 6,000 square feet. Homes would be one- and two-story structures with garages accessed from local streets, and architectural styles would include contemporary Craftsman, Spanish revival, and modern farmhouse to reflect existing neighborhood character while providing visual interest.

• Medium-Density Residential (MDR): Approximately 14.2 acres would be developed with a mix of small-lot single-family homes, duplexes, and townhomes at densities of 12–18 du/ac, for an estimated 186 units. These units would be clustered near the neighborhood commercial center and along the primary transit corridor to support walkability and transit use. Parking would be provided through a combination of tuck-under garages, small surface lots, and on-street spaces.

• High-Density Residential (HDR): Approximately 8.0 acres near the commercial core are designated for multi-family apartments at 25–35 du/ac, for up to 90 units. This area is envisioned as three- to four-story buildings with interior courtyards, ground-floor community rooms, and direct access to bike lanes and transit stops. A minimum of 15 percent of the HDR units (approximately 14 units) are anticipated to be reserved as deed-restricted affordable housing serving low- and moderate-income households in accordance with the City’s inclusionary housing ordinance.

COMMERCIAL AND OFFICE COMPONENT
The mixed-use neighborhood center is located near the intersection of Watt Avenue and the extended Riverbend Parkway and is designed to serve both project residents and surrounding neighborhoods:

• Neighborhood Commercial: Up to 120,000 square feet of commercial space is planned on approximately 11.3 acres, including a small-format grocery store (25,000–30,000 square feet), cafes and restaurants, neighborhood-serving retail (e.g., pharmacy, dry cleaner, fitness studio), and personal services. Buildings would generally be one to two stories in height, with active ground-floor frontages, outdoor seating, and pedestrian-oriented design.

• Office: Approximately 85,000 square feet of low- to mid-rise office space would be located on 5.2 acres adjacent to the commercial core, in one to three buildings ranging from two to four stories. Potential office tenants include medical and dental offices, professional services (e.g., engineering, legal, financial), and co-working spaces. The office component is projected to accommodate approximately 280 employees at buildout.

PARKS, OPEN SPACE, AND TRAILS
A defining feature of the project is its integrated parks and open space network, which is intended to provide both recreational opportunities and habitat preservation:

• Riverfront Community Park: A 10-acre public park along the American River would include multi-use turf fields, informal open lawns, a universally accessible playground, picnic areas with shade structures, a small amphitheater/terraced seating area for community events, restrooms, and a river overlook plaza with interpretive signage about the river’s ecology and cultural history.

• Neighborhood Parks: Two neighborhood parks (approximately 3–4 acres each) would be centrally located within the residential areas, providing play structures, half-court basketball, small lawn areas, community gardens, and shaded seating. These parks are intended to be within a 5-minute walk of most residential units.

• Trails and Greenways: The project includes a Class I multi-use trail along the riverfront that ties into the existing regional American River Parkway trail system, as well as internal greenway corridors with Class I and Class II facilities that connect residential neighborhoods to parks, the commercial core, and nearby schools. Bike lanes would be striped on key collector streets, and traffic-calming measures (e.g., narrowed travel lanes, enhanced crosswalks) would be incorporated to improve pedestrian and cyclist safety.

TRANSPORTATION AND CIRCULATION
The project would construct a new extension of Riverbend Parkway through the site, connecting from its existing terminus west of Watt Avenue to a new signalized intersection at Watt Avenue. This roadway would function as the primary east–west spine and include two travel lanes, a center turn lane or landscaped median in some segments, on-street parking near the commercial core, Class II bike lanes, and sidewalks on both sides.

Local residential streets would form a modified grid and curvilinear pattern, providing multiple access routes and reducing vehicle speeds. Two roundabouts are proposed at key internal intersections to manage traffic flow and enhance safety. The project would also construct bus pullouts and shelters along Watt Avenue and Riverbend Parkway to support existing and future transit service provided by the regional transit agency.

UTILITIES AND INFRASTRUCTURE
The project would connect to existing City water and sewer systems located in Watt Avenue and Riverbend Parkway. New on-site water distribution lines and sewer mains would be constructed within public rights-of-way and utility easements to serve the proposed uses. Stormwater would be managed through a combination of underground piping, bio-retention swales, and detention basins sized to meet current regional drainage standards and low impact development (LID) requirements. Electric, gas, and telecommunications infrastructure would be extended from adjacent developed areas, with distribution lines placed underground to the extent feasible.

PHASING
Buildout of the project is anticipated to occur over approximately 10–15 years in three major phases, beginning with backbone infrastructure (roadways, utilities, and initial drainage improvements), followed by residential neighborhoods and the neighborhood commercial center. The riverfront community park and key trail segments are intended to be delivered early in the buildout to provide community amenities and encourage active transportation from the outset.

DISCRETIONARY ACTIONS
Implementation of the project will require multiple discretionary approvals by the City of Riverbend, including adoption of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan, a General Plan Amendment to redesignate the site from Urban Reserve (UR) to a mix of Residential, Commercial, and Parks/Open Space land use categories, rezoning from AG-20 and OS to a combination of Specific Plan (SP), Residential, and Commercial zones, approval of a Planned Unit Development overlay, subdivision map(s), site plan and design review, a Development Agreement, and associated permits for grading, encroachment, and public improvements.

The Draft Environmental Impact Report (Draft EIR) evaluates the potential environmental impacts associated with construction and operation of the proposed project, identifies feasible mitigation measures, and considers project alternatives that could reduce or avoid significant impacts while still meeting most of the project objectives.
